1:
The chapter opens on the death of an Ammonite king and a courtesy that will kindle a war, and the witnesses set the scene from three directions. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott begins with the opening formula, “Now it came to pass after this,” which he notes is the very phrase used at 1 Chronicles 18 and carries no chronological significance; it simply marks the beginning of a fresh episode. The war with Ammon, only glanced at earlier in the book, is now to be told at length, and bound up with it the overthrow of Hadadezer will be related a second time, with additional details that Ellicott takes to betray a source other than the older account in 2 Samuel 10.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns from the formula to the heart of David’s conduct, and draws out of it a small treatise on gratitude. It becomes good people, he says, to be neighbourly and above all to be grateful; David will pay respect to Hanun because he is his neighbour, and religion teaches us to be civil and obliging to all and to do every office of kindness to those among whom we live, nor must difference in religion be any obstruction to it. But more than neighbourliness moves him: David remembered the kindness that Hanun’s father had shown him, and those who have received kindness must return it as they have opportunity — and return it to the children when the parents are gone. Henry adds the darker note the story will need, that, as the proverb of the ancients has it, wickedness proceeds from the wicked (1 Samuel 24:13): base men, designing ill themselves, are quick to suspect ill of others without cause, and it is such suspicion that will presently ruin the embassy.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke stands back and lays out the whole arc of the chapter as in a headnote: David sends a congratulatory message to Hanun; the messengers are treated with gross incivility; the Ammonites arm and hire thirty-two thousand chariots and besiege Medeba; Joab is sent and defeats them; the Syrians regroup and are broken a second time, and at last make a separate peace. For the opening words themselves Clarke is content to point the reader to the parallel, “see the same history” at 2 Samuel 10:1. The three voices do not contend; together they frame a war that begins, ironically, in an act of kindness.
2:
David’s first recorded act is one of remembered gratitude, and the single witness reads the verse for its small but telling departures from the parallel. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes at once a difference between the two accounts: the name Nahash, given here, is omitted in the older narrative, which supplies instead the name Hanun — omissions he thinks perhaps merely accidental on both sides. He recalls the history behind the name, for Saul’s very first campaign had been waged against this same Nahash, king of Ammon, in 1 Samuel 11; the kindness David now requites had been shown by an old enemy of Israel’s first king.

Ellicott lingers, as he so often does, over the diction. “The children of Ammon” he renders “the sons of Ammon,” a form like “the sons of Israel,” a title that calls attention to their tribal organisation. The little word “because” he would render “for,” where the older text has “according as.” And the phrase “shewed kindness to me” he marks as a Hebrew idiom that answers exactly to the Greek of Luke 1:72, where God performs the mercy promised to the fathers — so that David’s homely act of thanks is clothed, in the Hebrew, in the very language of covenant kindness. The whole verse, he judges, has been made more perspicuous than its counterpart in 2 Samuel 10:2 by a few slight changes and additions, the chronicler smoothing and clarifying the received report.

Brief as the note is, it lets the verse’s own movement stand out: David, secure on his throne, will send messengers to comfort a young king in his mourning, repaying a father’s kindness to the son. The messengers accordingly go down into the land of the children of Ammon, bearing the king’s comfort to Hanun in his bereavement, and the little clause “because his father shewed kindness to me” fixes the whole errand as a debt of gratitude repaid at the first opening. The reader who knows the sequel feels the pathos of it — that so generous an impulse, expressed in the language of covenant mercy, should be met with the suspicion and outrage the next verses record. Ellicott’s philology quietly underscores the theme the chapter turns on: a kindness offered in good faith, and flung back as an insult.
3:
No sooner have David’s envoys reached the Ammonite court than the princes pour suspicion into their young king’s ear, and the one witness fixes on the exact shape of their slander. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders their whispered accusation more literally than the English allows: not simply “Are not his servants come to search, and to overthrow, and to spy out the land?” but “Is it not for to search … that his servants are come unto thee?” — the emphasis falling on the sinister purpose imputed to the embassy. He judges the chronicler’s wording here hardly an improvement on the older account, which ran, “Is it not to search the city, and to spy it out, and to overthrow it, that David hath sent his servants unto thee?”

The difference, small as it looks, interests Ellicott, and he tracks it through the ancient versions. The older narrative speaks of the city — that is, Rabbath-Ammon, the Ammonite capital, the very place a spy would reconnoitre before a siege — while the text here speaks more broadly of the land. The Syriac and Arabic versions side with the older reading, “city”; the Septuagint and the Vulgate agree with the reading before us, “land.” Whether the princes feared for their capital or for their whole territory, the malice is the same.

Ellicott’s brief note leaves the verse’s moral plain enough. The princes read David’s condolence as espionage, and their suspicion is the hinge on which the whole disaster turns; a message of comfort is construed as a stratagem of war. It is the old pattern by which base minds, conscious of their own designs, impute the like to the innocent, and by so doing manufacture the very enmity they dread. The counsel they give will cost their nation dear, for it commits Hanun to an outrage against the persons of ambassadors that no king could overlook, and sets in motion the long and ruinous war that the rest of the chapter must tell. What began as a gesture of mourning is turned, by a whisper, into the pretext for a coalition and a slaughter.
4:
The insult, when Hanun offers it, is calculated and gross, and here two witnesses attend the scene. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the second half of the outrage, the cutting off of the garments “in the midst.” Following the Targum, Jarchi, and others, he explains that the mutilation reached usque ad pudenda — the robes were sheared away so as to leave exposed what nature and decency require to be concealed, a deliberate and shameful indignity, and he refers the reader to his fuller note at 2 Samuel 10:4.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parses the whole verse with his usual care. “Shaved them,” he notes, means — as the older account makes explicit — that Hanun shaved off the half of their beards, a peculiar humiliation in a world where the beard was the badge of a man’s dignity and freedom. The phrase the English renders “hard by their buttocks” he takes more literally as “unto the extremities,” and he remarks that the chronicler has here substituted a more decorous term than the blunter one that stands in the older narrative — a small instance of the reticence that marks this writer throughout. And the cutting off of the garments Ellicott reads as a device to make the envoys look like captives, stripped and half-naked, comparing the picture of shameful exposure in Isaiah 20:4, where the prophet foretells captives led away with their nakedness uncovered.

The two witnesses do not divide; between them they let the full weight of the affront appear. To shave the ambassadors of a friendly king, to strip them to their shame and send them home like prisoners of war, was to spit upon David’s kindness and to declare, past all recall, that Ammon would have war rather than peace. Clarke’s frankness about the indecency and Ellicott’s note on the chronicler’s decorous rewording together catch both the barbarity of the act and the delicacy with which the sacred historian records it. What Hanun meant as contempt becomes, in the story’s logic, the spark of his own destruction — an insult to the servants that their master will not leave unavenged.
5:
Word of the outrage runs ahead of the shamed men, and David’s first care is for the envoys themselves. The single witness reads the verse for both its feeling and its philology. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the word rendered “ashamed” is not the ordinary term for shame, but a stronger one meaning confounded — a word whose root sense, he says, is to be pricked or wounded, so that the men are not merely embarrassed but cut to the quick. He compares its use in Psalms 35:4, where it forms a climax to the milder word, heaping confusion upon shame.

The phrase “till your beards be grown,” Ellicott adds, uses a verb meaning to sprout or shoot, the same word used in Judges 16:22 of the hair of Samson’s head beginning to grow again after it was shaven. The mutilated beards would in time return; the men need only wait, and the shame of the shaven half-beard, so keenly felt in that world, would heal of itself with the passing weeks. And he places the scene geographically: Jericho, where David bids them tarry, lay on their road up to the capital, a convenient halting-place where they might recover their dignity out of the public eye before presenting themselves at court.

Slight though the note is, it opens the tenderness of the verse. When word came of how his men had been served, David sent to meet them on the road, unwilling that they should come up to the court in their disgrace; he does not upbraid his humiliated servants nor hurry them into the city to be stared at, but shelters them at Jericho until the visible marks of their shame are healed. It is the conduct of a master who feels his servants’ shame as his own — a small mercy set against Hanun’s great cruelty, and a quiet earnest of the protection a good lord owes those who suffer in his service. The delicacy Ellicott finds in the Hebrew, weighing the very word for their confusion, gives the verse its human warmth, even as it prepares the reader for the war that this studied insult has now made inevitable.
6:
Too late for peace, the Ammonites reckon the cost of their insult and reach for hired swords, and here the fullest company of witnesses gathers. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws five lessons from the verse and its sequel. First, he marvels at how the hearts of sinners marked for ruin are hardened to their own destruction: the Ammonites saw that they had made themselves odious to David, and wisdom would have counselled them to sue for peace and offer satisfaction, the rather because they had made themselves obnoxious not only to David but to the justice of God, who is King of nations and will maintain the violated laws of nations; instead they prepared for war, and so brought upon themselves the desolations David never intended them. Then he notes how the courage of brave men is heightened by difficulty, as Joab’s will be; how vain the greatest art and strength are against justice; how little those profit who rally again without God on their side; and how those who meddle in a strife not their own do well at last to learn wisdom and meddle no further.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds a soldier’s observation about the reinforcements: the chariots and horsemen from Mesopotamia, he points out, are not mentioned in the older parallel account, and he conjectures that they did not arrive until after the Ammonites and their first allies had already been beaten in the opening battle.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the verse as the point where this narrative begins to diverge from the older one and to draw on an independent source. “Made themselves odious,” he explains, is literally “had become in bad odour,” an unusual Aramaized form of the verb used in the parallel. The “thousand talents of silver” — a sum he reckons at some four hundred thousand pounds — is a detail peculiar to the Chronicles, the talent being a weight and not a coin, for coined money was as yet unknown. He identifies the hired peoples: Aram-naharaim, the country between the two rivers; Aram-maachah, the realm of the king of Maachah; and Zobah, the Aram-zobah of the older account; and he notes that the Syriac and Arabic here name Haran, Nisibis, and Edom among the sources of the mercenary host. The witnesses do not divide; together they show a nation multiplying its allies against David and, in the very act, multiplying its ruin.
7:
The muster the Ammonite silver has bought is enormous, and the witnesses linger over its arithmetic and its meaning. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the figure beside the older account: there the hired host is numbered as twenty thousand from Beth-rehob and Zoba, a thousand of King Maachah’s men, and twelve thousand of Ish-tob — some thirty-three thousand in all, with no mention there of chariots or cavalry; and these, he adds, could not have been the whole army. His fellow wesleyan-arminian John Wesley construes the “thirty and two thousand chariots” as thirty-two thousand men who fought partly from chariots and partly on foot, attending upon the chariots, as the ancient manner of fighting was.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the number hardest. The chronicler’s total — thirty-two thousand plus the men of Maachah — is, he shows, in substantial accord with the older reckoning of thirty-two thousand plus a thousand Maachathites. But the expression “thirty-two thousand chariotry” is not to be pressed: the writer wished to lay stress on the chariots and cavalry as the chief arm of the Aramaean states, and to be as concise as possible. That he did not mean thirty-two thousand chariots literally is clear, Ellicott argues, both because he must have known an army does not consist of chariots only, and because at 1 Chronicles 18:4 he had already given the army of Zobah its natural proportions of chariots, horsemen, and foot. The present text of the older account can hardly be right, he adds, since it makes the whole army infantry; and the great plains of Aram were a natural training-ground for horsemen and charioteers.

Ellicott notes two further details peculiar to this account: that the allied host came and pitched before Medeba, a town he locates south-east of Heshbon at a site now called Madibiya, and that the muster of the Ammonites themselves, joining their hired allies, goes unmentioned in the older narrative. The witnesses thus circle a real difficulty in the numbers without wholly resolving it, agreeing that the figure is meant to impress rather than to be counted, and that the two accounts, however they differ in their tallies, describe the same overwhelming coalition drawn up against Israel.
8:
David’s answer to the gathering coalition is to commit his whole striking force, and the single witness pauses over a nicety in the way that force is named. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the phrase “all the host of the mighty men” stands so in the Hebrew text, while the Hebrew margin and the older parallel account read simply “all the host,” taking “the mighty men” in apposition. The “mighty men,” he explains, were a special corps — the famed gibborim, David’s picked warriors — and he gathers a string of places, from 1 Samuel 23:13 to 1 Kings 1:8, where the term denotes that elite body of veterans who had followed David from his outlaw days and formed the backbone of his army. Either, then, the word here has a looser, more general sense, embracing the whole host, or we must read “and the mighty men,” distinguishing the corps of veterans from the general levy.

Small as the point is, it lets the verse’s gravity show. David does not treat the Ammonite affront as a border skirmish to be handled by a captain of the guard; he sends Joab, his commander-in-chief, and with him the full weight of Israel’s army and the flower of its warriors. Where the reader might have expected a mere reprisal, the text speaks of “all the host,” the entire fighting strength of the nation, marching under its foremost captain against the Ammonite capital. The insult to the ambassadors has become a war of the kingdom, and the king throws his whole military strength into it.

Ellicott’s grammatical hesitation — whether “the mighty men” names the entire army or only its veteran core — does not obscure the plain sense of the verse: the disgrace done to David’s servants is now to be answered by the massed power of David’s throne, and the two hosts, the hired coalition of the north and the gathered might of Israel, are set on their collision course. The single witness offers no more than a note on the syntax, but that note quietly measures the scale of what is coming, as the whole levy of Israel marches out under Joab to meet the mercenary armies that the Ammonites have bought.
9:
The armies take up their ground, and the one witness reads the disposition with a topographer’s eye. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders “before the gate of the city” more literally as “in the outlet of the city,” noting that the older account has “in the outlet of the gate”; the city in question, he judges, is Medeba, named a few verses before. The Ammonites, that is, drew up their own army close under their walls, at the point where the roads issued from the town.

The hired kings, by contrast, took their stand apart. Where the English says they “were” in the field, Ellicott supplies from the previous clause the fuller sense, “put the battle in array,” and he recalls that the older account here repeats their names — Aram-zobah and Rehob, the men of Tob and Maachah. “In the field” he explains as the open country, the level plain — the mishor — of Medeba, named in Joshua 13:9, a tableland where there was room for the free movement of cavalry and chariots. The geography, in his reading, is the key to the whole battle: the Ammonite foot massed at the gate, the Aramaean chariotry deployed on the open plain behind, so that any force attacking the one laid its back open to the other. The choice of the level tableland was the mercenaries' own, for on the open plain their horses and cars could manoeuvre and charge, where among the walls and roads of the town they would have been hemmed and blunted.

Ellicott’s brief, exact note thus does more than gloss a phrase; it sets the stage for Joab’s dilemma in the verse to come. The allies have chosen their ground shrewdly, the townsmen at their walls and the mercenaries in the plain where their horses and cars can tell, and Israel’s general finds himself between two hosts. The witness leaves the reader looking, as it were, over Joab’s shoulder at a field cunningly arranged to catch an army between the gate and the open country, and so prepares the tactical crisis that the next verses will resolve.
10:
Joab finds himself caught in a vice, and the single witness follows his generalship move by move. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders “the battle was set against him before and behind” more literally as “the front of the battle had become towards him, front and rear” — Joab is threatened simultaneously from two directions, the order of the Hebrew words differing slightly from the older account. He explains the predicament in plain military terms: the Ammonites lay in front, close under their city, while their Aramaean allies stood at some distance in the plain behind; for Joab to throw his whole army against either enemy would be to bare his rear to the assault of the other. He had, therefore, no choice but to divide his forces.

The division is deliberate and shrewd. “The choice of Israel,” Ellicott notes, is literally the chosen or young warriors — a collective singular — the picked men of the Israelite army; and these Joab took under his own command and led against the Aramaeans, judging the hired chariotry of the north the more dangerous foe. The remainder he committed to his brother Abishai to face the Ammonites at the gate. “Put them in array,” he adds, is better rendered “set the battle in array” or “drew up against,” the same Hebrew phrase that will recur in the next verse.

Ellicott’s careful gloss turns the bare narrative into a lesson in command. Surrounded, outnumbered, threatened front and rear, Joab does not panic or retreat but reads the field, reckons which enemy is the graver threat, splits his army into two answering wings, and reserves the flower of his troops for the harder fight. It is the conduct of a soldier who keeps his head when caught between two fires, and all of it improvised on the instant, with the enemy already closing on both sides and no leisure for a council of war. The witness leaves the two divisions poised — Joab and the chosen men wheeling toward the Syrians in the plain, Abishai forming his line against the Ammonites at the wall — and the reader waits to see whether courage and generalship, under God, can turn a trap into a victory.
11:
With Joab’s own division wheeling toward the Syrians, the second half of the army passes to his brother, and the single witness marks a small grammatical seam in the record. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes only that where the text says of Abishai’s men “they set themselves in array,” the older parallel account has the verb in the singular — as it likewise did in the previous verse — the chronicler preferring the plural where the earlier writer used the singular. It is one of the many slight, almost imperceptible variations by which this later narrative departs from its source without altering the sense.

Brief as the note is, the verse it glosses completes the picture the last verse began. Joab has taken the choice of Israel against the more formidable Aramaean chariotry in the open plain; now the rest of the people are delivered into the hand of Abishai, drawn up against the children of Ammon before their city gate. The trap that had closed on Israel front and rear is answered by a matching division: two brothers, two wings, each facing one horn of the enemy’s crescent, so that neither host can fall upon an unguarded back. The encirclement that had threatened to crush Israel is thus turned, by a single swift decision, into two separate and winnable battles. The singular verb of the older account, which speaks of the division as a single ordered body, and the plural here, which lets the individual soldiers take their stations, catch the same disposition from two angles — the one army seen as a whole, the many men seen taking their places in the line.

Ellicott does not labour the point, and there is little more to be said of so plain a verse; but its very brevity serves the narrative’s momentum. The lines are formed, the commanders set, the field divided between the brothers, and everything now waits upon the compact and the charge that the following verses record. The chronicler’s small change of number is the sort of quiet fidelity to his own idiom that, verse upon verse, distinguishes his retelling from the account he follows, even where, as here, the substance is identical.
12:
Before the trumpets sound, Joab binds himself and his brother in a soldiers’ compact, and the single witness finds a note of theology hidden in its wording. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the agreement literally: “If Aram prevail over me, thou shalt become to me for succour; and if the children of Ammon prevail over thee, then I will succour thee.” The mutual pledge is plain enough — each brother stands ready to reinforce the other should his own wing be overpowered — but Ellicott lingers over the word chosen for “succour.” It is teshu‘ah, he explains, a less frequent synonym of the more familiar yeshu‘ah, which is the very term that stands in the older account; and this word, with its cognate noun, is the ordinary Hebrew for saving and for salvation.

The observation is small but suggestive. The help the brothers promise one another is named by a word that Scripture elsewhere reserves for the deliverance God works for his people; the “succour” of the battlefield and the “salvation” of the sanctuary are, in the Hebrew, one and the same root. So even in Joab’s practical arrangement for mutual support there sounds, faintly, the language of the greater deliverance — a hint that the victory about to be won, for all the generalship that orders these two wings, will be reckoned at last not to the strength of the brothers but to the God who saves.

Ellicott does not press the point beyond the philology, and the verse’s plain sense stands: two commanders, each with an enemy to his front, agree that the stronger shall fly to the aid of the harder pressed, so that the divided army may yet fight as one. It is prudent, level-headed, and brave, the arrangement of men who have measured their danger and mean to meet it together; that the weaker-pressed should fly to the other’s aid is the plainest wisdom of a divided command, keeping the two wings, for all their separation, effectively one army under one purpose. But the word “succour” leans forward to the confession Joab is about to make aloud in the next verse — that the issue, when all their planning is done, lies in the hand of the LORD.
13:
The general’s charge to his men rises, at its close, into prayer, and the witnesses gather about the words that the passage sets at its centre. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, for the exhortation “Be of good courage,” simply directs the reader to his fuller note on the older account at 2 Samuel 10:12, where the same charge stands.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott parses the sentence with care and love. “Be of good courage,” he notes, renders the same verb translated “be strong” in the previous verse, and “let us behave ourselves valiantly” is that verb again, now in a reflexive form; so that the whole runs literally, “Be strong, and let us show ourselves strong!” — a ringing summons to hearten one another. But it is the final clause that Ellicott dwells on. “Let the LORD do that which is good in his sight” he renders more literally still, “And Jehovah — the good in his own eyes may he do!” — and he observes that the chronicler has laid a deliberate stress on the auspicious word “good,” as well as an emphasis on the name Jehovah, leaving the whole issue in the hands of him who is Lord of hosts and God of battles. The verb, he adds, expresses a pious wish that the right may not miscarry.

Ellicott draws the striking conclusion that the spirit which breathes in the Lord’s own prayer, “Thy will be done,” was not unknown to the warriors of the old theocracy. Here, on the eve of battle, with the army divided and the enemy pressing front and rear, Joab neither boasts of his strength nor despairs of it, but couples a soldier’s resolve — be strong, and let us show ourselves strong — with a believer’s submission: whatever pleases God, let God do. Courage and resignation meet in a single breath; the men are to fight as if all depended on their valour, and to leave the outcome as if all depended on God. The witnesses do not divide, and together they let this verse stand at the theological heart of the whole war — the point where military prudence bows to the will of heaven, and the battle is committed, before it is joined, into the hand of the God who will do what is good in his sight.
14:
The battle is joined, and it is over almost before it begins, and the single witness offers only a small grammatical correction. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the phrase “before the Syrians,” where Joab and his people draw near to the fight, is better rendered “against Aram” — the older account, he says, uses here the more classical construction — and he adds that the preposition employed is the same word rendered “to meet” at 1 Chronicles 12:17. Joab does not merely approach the Syrians; he advances upon them, to close and to engage.

Slight though the note is, it fits the sudden collapse the verse records. Joab, with the chosen men of Israel, presses forward against the Aramaean chariotry drawn up in the plain — the enemy he had judged the more dangerous — and the mercenaries, for all their numbers and their vaunted horse and cars, break and flee before him. The hired coalition that the Ammonite silver had assembled, thirty thousand and more, does not stand; at the first determined onset it turns and runs. The witness supplies no comment on the rout itself, and there is little in so bare a verse for the commentator to expound; but the correction he makes — “against,” not merely “before” — quietly underscores the initiative that carried the day. It was Joab who took the field to the enemy, and the enemy who would not abide his coming. The vaunted horse and cars of Aram, on which the Ammonites had spent a thousand talents of silver, availed nothing against a resolute charge, and the mercenaries earned their pay by flight.

Behind the flight of the Syrians lies, for the reader who has followed the chapter, the confession just uttered: be strong, and let the LORD do what is good in his sight. The soldiers fought; the mercenaries fled; and the good that God saw fit to do was, in this hour, the swift scattering of the greater host. The philologist’s small adjustment of a preposition thus opens, almost in passing, onto the whole theology of the war — that Israel advances, and God disposes the flight of her enemies.
15:
The rout of the mercenaries breaks the nerve of those who hired them, and the single witness marks how differently the chronicler has framed this turn. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott observes that the Hebrew construction opening the verse is quite unlike that at the muster earlier in the chapter; it should be rendered, he says, “Now the sons of Ammon had seen that Aram was routed” — a backward glance rather than a fresh action, catching the moment the townsmen watched their allies break. When the Ammonites saw the Aramaean line collapse before Joab, they too fled before Abishai his brother and withdrew within the city.

Ellicott notes several small marks of the chronicler’s hand. The words “they likewise” are an explanatory addition to the text as it stands in the older account, spelling out that the Ammonites did as their mercenaries had done; and “his brother,” identifying Abishai, is a like clarifying touch. The closing notice, that Joab then came to Jerusalem, he calls an abridgment of the fuller older narrative, the chronicler compressing the general’s return into a single clause.

The verse completes the double victory that Joab’s division of his army had made possible. The plan laid in the face of encirclement has worked exactly as intended: the choice of Israel has shattered the Syrians in the plain, and the sight of that collapse has emptied the Ammonites of all will to fight, so that Abishai’s wing needs only to advance for the enemy before it to give way and bolt for the shelter of the walls. The hirelings had fought for money and fled when the day turned, and the Ammonites, seeing their bought courage evaporate, had no heart to stand alone against Israel. Neither brother has had to rescue the other; the compact of mutual succour proved unneeded, for both wings prevailed. Ellicott’s quiet attention to the chronicler’s additions and abridgments lets the reader see the historian at his work, shaping the received account to his own ends, even as the narrative itself records a first campaign ending in the flight of both the hired host and the nation that hired it.
16:
Beaten but unwilling to accept it, the Syrians send beyond the great river for fresh armies, and the single witness unfolds the geography and the names. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott first marks a difference from the older account, which says that Hadarezer “sent and drew forth” his forces; and he stresses that this name, Hadarezer — the Hadadezer of the earlier chapter — is important, for it helps to identify this campaign with the war already related at 1 Chronicles 18:3 and following.

The phrase “beyond the river” sends Ellicott into a long note on the Euphrates. The name, he explains, was Purat or Puratu to the Babylonians and Assyrians, Furat to the Arabs, Ufratus to the ancient Persians, and derives from the Accadian Pura-nunu, “the great river”; the Hebrew form is simply this word with a feminine ending, so that the famous name means “The River” par excellence, as at Genesis 15:18 and Isaiah 8:7. And he draws a shrewd inference from the idiom itself: the very use of the phrase “beyond the river” to fix the position of the eastern Aramaeans shows that the narrative the chronicler is here borrowing was originally written in Palestine, west of the Euphrates, from whose standpoint those states lay “beyond.”

Ellicott adds that the Syriac and Arabic versions insert here the note that the Syrians “came to Hilam,” the Helam of the older account named in the next verse. The captain of the host, Shophach, appears in the older narrative as Shobach — the letters p and b being much alike in Hebrew — and Ellicott compares the Arabic safaka, “to shed blood,” a fitting name for a general. “Went before them,” he explains, means “commanded them”; and from the fact that these trans-Euphrates states marched at Hadarezer’s summons, he concludes that his suzerainty was acknowledged by Aramaean powers lying east of the great river. The witness thus turns a bare notice of reinforcement into a small map of the Aramaean world, and fastens the whole episode, by the name of Hadarezer, to the wider war of the preceding chapter. A defeated coalition, refusing to own its defeat, reaches across the great river for one more throw of the dice.
17:
David will not leave the second Syrian war to a lieutenant, and the single witness dwells on the mysterious place-name that the chronicler has quietly removed. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that where our verse says David “came upon them,” the older account says he “came to Helam”; the chronicler, he suggests, has substituted an intelligible phrase for the name of a locality that had become obscure. He records a striking conjecture pressed upon him privately by Professor Sayce — that this mysterious Helam is none other than Aleppo, the Halman of the Assyrian monuments — though he sets it down as a suggestion rather than a settled identification.

Ellicott then works at the words themselves. “Upon them … against them” is, he says, literally “unto them,” a simple prepositional phrase; and the Hebrew for “to Helam” contains the very same consonants as that phrase, with but one letter more. It may be, he ventures, that the older account’s “to Helam” was itself understood, or misunderstood, as a common noun meaning “to their army” — from a Hebrew word for a host or force — rather than as a proper name at all. The following clause, “so when David had put the battle in array against the Syrians,” he judges a needless repetition of what precedes, and thinks the older account probably preserves the better reading: “And Aram put the battle in array against David.”

So the debate over the field’s very name is left genuinely open. Was there a place called Helam, perhaps the great city of Aleppo, where the armies met? Or does the name conceal only a common word for “their army,” a scribe’s or a translator’s ghost? Ellicott holds the possibilities in balance and does not decide. What is not in doubt is the escalation the verse records: David gathers all Israel, crosses the Jordan, and comes in person against the reinforced Syrian host — no longer a punitive expedition under Joab against Ammon and its hirelings, but the king himself leading the nation’s full array against the massed power of Aram beyond the river. Whatever the true name of the field, the stakes have risen from a border reprisal to a reckoning between kingdoms.
18:
The second battle ends as the first had, in a Syrian rout, and the two witnesses weigh its casualty list and the vexed numbers within it. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, on the “forty thousand footmen,” refers the reader to his note on the older account at 2 Samuel 10:18, where he had set out how the differing figures are to be reconciled.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the two accounts frankly side by side. Our verse reads, literally, “seven thousand chariotry” and forty thousand footmen; the older narrative reads “seven hundred chariots, and forty thousand horsemen.” Such deviations, Ellicott judges, seem to point to independent sources rather than to simple copying, and between the two accounts we can hardly choose — though he thinks “horsemen” may well be the more correct reading against our text’s “footmen,” comparing the proportions of chariots, cavalry, and foot given at 1 Chronicles 18:4. The slaying of Shophach, the Syrian captain, he notes as an abridged statement of what the older account tells more fully.

Here the numbers open a real difference of method between the witnesses. Clarke, in the manner of the harmonist, is content to reconcile the seven hundred chariots of the one account with the seven thousand of the other and to leave the figures standing as complementary; Ellicott, more the textual critic, takes the divergences — seven hundred against seven thousand, horsemen against footmen — as evidence that the chronicler drew on a source distinct from the older narrative, and declines to harmonize what he thinks two genuinely independent reports. Yet neither doubts the substance: the reinforced Syrian army, gathered from beyond the Euphrates under Hadarezer’s general, was broken in the open field by David in person, its chariotry and its foot cut down in their thousands and its commander slain. The precise tally may be past recovering; the completeness of the defeat is not in question, and with it the power of Aram to sustain the Ammonite cause is effectively ended. The mercenaries who had crossed the river to retrieve their honour lost instead their general and the better part of their host, and the Syrian threat that had shadowed the whole war was, at a stroke, removed.
19:
With their overlord’s cause broken past mending, the vassals of Hadarezer change masters, and the witnesses read the chapter’s close as the submission of the conquered. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, on their making peace and becoming David’s servants, points the reader to his note on the older account at 2 Samuel 10:19, and to his remarks on the omissions that mark the books of Chronicles generally.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the verse against its parallel with his usual precision. “When the servants of Hadarezer saw that they were put to the worse,” he notes, is given more fully in the older account as “all the kings, servants of Hadarezer”; these tributaries of the Syrian overlord now transferred their fealty to David. Where our verse says they “made peace with David,” the older account says “with Israel”; and where ours says they “became his servants” — literally, “served him” — the older says “served them.” The difference is slight but characteristic: to the earlier writer God’s people is the main topic, so the Syrians serve Israel; to the chronicler it is the divinely anointed king, so they serve David. The closing clause, “neither would the Syrians help the children of Ammon any more,” Ellicott renders “Aram was not willing to come to the help of the sons of Ammon,” where the older account had written that Aram “feared” to come — the one stressing unwillingness, the other fear.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the moral of the whole reversal. Those who have meddled in a quarrel not their own, he says, do well to learn wisdom at last and meddle no further; the Syrians, finding Israel the conquering side, not only broke off their alliance with Ammon but made their peace with David and became his servants. And in this Henry hears a parable: let those who have in vain stood out against God be thus wise for themselves, and agree with him quickly while they are in the way, and become his servants — for they cannot but see themselves undone if they remain his enemies. So the chapter that began with an insult to David’s kindness ends with his enemies at his feet, and the witnesses leave the reader with the counsel that the only safety of sinners lies in submitting to the Lord and seeking peace with him.
